1: YHWH ʾal-bĕqeṣpĕkā tôkîḥēnî ûbăḥămātĕkā tĕyassĕrēnî (YHWH ʾal-bĕqeṣpĕkā ṯôḵîḥēnî ûḇăḥămāṯĕḵā ṯĕyassĕrēnî): 'O LORD, rebuke me not in your anger, nor discipline me in your wrath.' Psalm 38 is one of the seven penitential psalms (with Pss 6, 32, 51, 102, 130, 143) — psalms of confession and prayer for forgiveness. The opening, nearly identical to Psalms 6:1, places the psalm in the tradition of prayer for divine restraint of punitive wrath. The two verbs — yākōḥ ('to rebuke, correct') and yāsar ('to discipline, chasten') — are both pedagogical: divine correction is real but potentially excessive. The psalmist does not deny deserving correction but prays that the wrath (qeṣep, ḥēmâ) be moderated: chastening, yes; annihilation, no.
2: kî ḥiṣṣêkā nēḥŝtu-bî ûtappîl ʿālay yādekā (kî ḥiṣṣêḵā nēḥŝtu-ḇî ûṯappîl ʿālay yāḏeḵā): 'For your arrows have sunk into me, and your hand has come down on me.' The arrows (ḥēṣ) of divine judgment — a metaphor for the painful circumstances (illness, loss) the psalmist experiences as divine response to sin — have nēḥŝtu ('sunk in, been driven'). The phrase ûtappîl ʿālay yādekā, 'and your hand presses down on me,' uses the hiphil of nāpal ('to fall, cast down') with the intensive sense of heavy pressure. YHWH's hand on the psalmist produces the physical symptoms described in Psalms 38:3-8 — the suffering is attributed to divine action, not random misfortune.
3: ʾên mĕtōm biśĕrî mippĕnê zaʿmekā ʾên šālôm baʿăṣāmay mippĕnê ḥaṭṭāʾtî (ʾên mĕṯōm biśĕrî mipp̄ĕnê zaʿmeḵā ʾên šālôm baʿăṣāmay mipp̄ĕnê ḥaṭṭāʾṯî): 'There is no soundness in my flesh because of your indignation; there is no health in my bones because of my sin.' The double ʾên ... ʾên ('there is no ... there is no') creates total privation: no mĕtōm ('soundness, wholeness') in the flesh (bāśār), no šālôm ('peace, health') in the bones (ʿeṣem). Both diagnoses are given double causation: mippĕnê zaʿmekā ('because of your indignation') and mippĕnê ḥaṭṭāʾtî ('because of my sin'). The psalmist refuses to separate physical suffering from theological cause: the illness is both the result of sin and the experience of divine displeasure.
4: kî ʿăwōnōtay ʿābĕrû rōʾšî kĕmaśśāʾ kābēd yikbĕdû mimmennî (kî ʿăwōnōṯay ʿāḇĕrû rōʾšî kĕmaśśāʾ kāḇēḏ yiḵbĕḏû mimmennî): 'For my iniquities have gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me.' The spatial metaphor ʿābar rōʾš ('pass over the head') describes overwhelming: the iniquities (ʿăwōnōt) have risen above the psalmist's capacity to manage, like floodwaters that submerge. The simile kĕmaśśāʾ kābēd ('like a heavy burden') — a load heavier than the bearer — captures the crushing weight. The verb yiḵbĕdû ('they are heavy, they press') from kābēd ('to be heavy, honored') — the same root as kābôd ('glory/weight') — inverts honor: what should be light (forgiven sin) becomes crushing (unforgiven guilt).
5: hibʾîšû nāmaqû ḥabbûrōtāy mippĕnê ʾiwwaltî (hiḇʾîšû nāmaqû ḥabbûrôṯāy mipp̄ĕnê ʾiwwaltî): 'My wounds stink and fester because of my foolishness.' The verb bāʾaš ('to stink, be offensive') in the hiphil hiḇʾîšû ('they have made to stink') — combined with nāmaqû ('they are putrid, have rotted') — describes infected wounds whose stench signals advanced corruption. The word ḥabbûrôt ('wounds, bruises, stripes') are the physical marks; mippĕnê ʾiwwaltî, 'because of my foolishness,' names the source. The noun ʾiwwelet ('foolishness') in Proverbs is the opposite of ḥokmâ ('wisdom') — the fool acts without regard for consequences or covenant, and the festering wounds are the embodied consequence.
6: naʿăwêtî šaḥōtî ʿad-mĕʾōd kol-hayyôm qōdēr hibbāltî (naʿăwêṯî šaḥōṯî ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ ḵol-hayyôm qōḏēr hibbālṯî): 'I am utterly bowed down and prostrate; all the day I go about mourning.' The verb ʿāwâ (niphal naʿăwêtî, 'I am bowed, twisted, bent over') captures the physical posture of someone whose weight of suffering prevents standing erect. The intensifiers — šāḥôtî ʿad-mĕʾōd ('bowed down exceedingly') — amplify. The temporal phrase kol-hayyôm ('all the day') eliminates any interval of relief. The participle qōdēr ('darkened, in dark mourning garments') describes the public appearance of grief: the psalmist walks about visibly in mourning, unable to conceal his state.
7: kî ḵeślay mālĕʾû niqeh ûʾên mĕtōm biśĕrî (kî ḵeślay māleʾû niqeh ûʾên mĕṯōm biśĕrî): 'For my loins are filled with burning, and there is no soundness in my flesh.' The noun kesālayim ('loins, the lumbar/kidney region') and the verbal niqeh (from nilqad or related — 'burning, inflammation') describe severe internal pain. The repeated ʾên mĕtōm biśĕrî ('no soundness in my flesh') from Psalms 38:3 — the inclusio of bodily suffering — underscores that the illness is total: no part of the body has escaped the divine correction. The psalmist's entire physical being is the medium of penitential experience.
8: pûgôtî wĕnidkêtî ʿad-mĕʾōd šāʾagtî minnehămat libbî (p̄ûḡôṯî wĕniḏkêṯî ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ šāʾaḡtî minnehămaṯ libbî): 'I am feeble and crushed; I groan because of the tumult of my heart.' The verb pûg ('to be numb, feeble, exhausted') combined with dākkâ (niphal niḏkêṯî, 'I am crushed, pulverized') — the same root as dakkāʾ in Psalms 34:18 ('crushed in spirit') — describe the inner dissolution of one who has reached physical and emotional exhaustion. The audible response: šāʾagtî ('I roar, groan') minnehămat libbî, 'because of the agitation/turmoil of my heart.' The lion-roar šāʾag applied to human anguish conveys the involuntary, raw vocalisation of extreme distress — what Hezekiah's prayer in Isaiah 38:14 calls 'chattering like a swallow.'
9: ʾădōnāy nĕgdĕkā kol-taʾăwātî wĕʾanḥātî mimmĕkā lōʾ-nistārâ (ʾăḏōnāy neḡḏeḵā ḵol-taʾăwāṯî wĕʾanḥāṯî mimmĕḵā lōʾ-nistārâ): 'O Lord, all my longing is before you; my sighing is not hidden from you.' The divine address ʾădōnāy ('my Lord') in direct speech — after the third-person description of suffering — personalizes the prayer. The phrase nĕgdĕkā kol-taʾăwātî, 'before you is all my longing,' uses neged ('in front of, before') to assert that YHWH sees the psalmist's deepest desire. The groaning (ʾanḥâ) that the heart produces (Psalms 38:8) is lōʾ-nistārâ ('not hidden') from YHWH — nothing is concealed. The reassurance: even the inarticulate suffering of Psalms 38:8 reaches God as fully as any formulated prayer.
10: libbî sĕḥar koḥî ʿăzābānî wĕʾôr-ʿênay gam-hēm ʾên ʾittî (libbî sĕḥar ḵōḥî ʿăzāḇānî wĕʾôr-ʿênay ḡam-hēm ʾên ʾittî): 'My heart throbs; my strength fails me, and the light of my eyes — it also has gone from me.' The verb sāḥar (here 'throb, race') describes the heart's physical distress — palpitations from illness or anxiety. The phrase kōḥî ʿăzābānî ('my strength has abandoned me') uses ʿāzab ('to leave, forsake') — the same verb used of YHWH's forsaking in Psalms 22:1. Physical exhaustion is framed as abandonment. The most poignant element: wĕʾôr-ʿênay gam-hēm ʾên ʾittî, 'even the light of my eyes has left me' — the eyes that once saw light are darkened. Complete sensory and physical failure accompanies the spiritual crisis.
11: ʾōhăbay wĕrēʿay minneged negʿî yaʿamōdû ûqĕrôbay mērāḥōq ʿāmādû (ʾōhăḇay wĕrēʿay minnegeḏ negʿî yaʿămōḏû ûqĕrôḇay mērāḥôq ʿāmāḏû): 'My friends and companions stand aloof from my plague, and my nearest kin stand far off.' The social dimension of suffering: ʾōhăbay ('my lovers, those who love me'), rēʿay ('my companions'), and qĕrôbay ('my near ones, relatives') all maintain distance. The phrase minneged negʿî ('from the presence of my plague') — the wound/disease is the reason for their withdrawal — suggests that the psalmist's suffering is visible and repulsive. The spatial opposites yaʿamōdû ('they stand') and mērāḥōq ('at a distance') echo the social abandonment motif of Psalms 22:11 and the suffering servant's social isolation in Isaiah 53:3. The abandonment by intimates is a recognizable form of human suffering that Jesus also experienced (Mark 14:50).
12: waynĕqĕšû mĕbaqqĕšê napšî wĕdōrĕšê rāʿātî dibbĕrû hawwôt (waynĕqĕšû mĕḇaqqĕšê napšî wĕḏōrĕšê rāʿāṯî dibbĕrû hawwôṯ): 'Those who seek my life lay their snares; those who seek my hurt speak of ruin and meditate treachery all the day long.' The contrast to the distant friends (Psalms 38:11): the enemies are actively engaged. mĕbaqqĕšê napšî ('those who seek my life') lay traps (nāqaš, 'to snare'); dōrĕšê rāʿātî ('those who seek my harm') speak hawwôt ('ruination, disaster'). The phrase ûmirmôt kol-hayyôm yehgû, 'and meditate deceit all the day,' uses hāgâ from Psalms 1:2 — the daily meditation on Torah inverted into daily scheming of deceit. What the righteous meditates on (Torah) determines character; what the wicked meditates on (mirmâ) determines destruction.
13: waʾănî kĕḥērēš lōʾ ʾĕšmaʿ ûkĕ'illēm lōʾ yiptaḥ pîw (waʾănî ḵĕḥērēš lōʾ ʾĕšmaʿ ûḵĕ'illēm lōʾ yip̄taḥ pîw): 'But I am like a deaf man; I do not hear, like a mute man who does not open his mouth.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but I') introduces the psalmist's chosen response to the enemies: silence. The similes kĕḥērēš ('like a deaf one') and kĕ'illēm ('like a mute one') describe a deliberate non-engagement. The psalmist neither hears the enemy's slanders nor responds to them — a profound counterintuitive strategy. Isaiah 53:7 applies a nearly identical posture to the Servant: 'like a sheep that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth.' Matthew 26:63 and 27:14 note that Jesus was silent before his accusers, fulfilling this prophetic-psalmist pattern.
14: waʾĕhî kĕʾîš ʾăšer lōʾ šômēaʿ ûʾên bĕpîw tôkāḥôt (waʾĕhî ḵĕʾîš ʾăšer lōʾ šômēaʿ ûʾên bĕp̄îw tôḵāḥôṯ): 'So I have become like a man who does not hear, and in whose mouth are no rebukes.' The continuation of Psalms 38:13: the psalmist's silence extends to the absence of tôkāḥôt ('rebukes, counter-arguments') in his mouth. The word tôkāḥat is the technical term for the reasoned argument in a legal dispute — the psalmist has no counter-charges, no legal defense, no retaliatory rebuke. This absolute silence before accusation is one of the most remarkable postures in the Psalter. The choice to be silent rather than self-defend reflects trust in YHWH as the true advocate (cf. Psalms 38:15).
15: kî-lĕkā YHWH hôḥāltî ʾattâ taʿăneh ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhāy (kî-lĕḵā YHWH hôḥāltî ʾattâ taʿăneh ʾăḏōnāy ʾĕlōhāy): 'For it is for you, O LORD, that I wait; it is you, O Lord my God, who will answer.' The silence of Psalms 38:13-14 is explained here: the psalmist is silent before humans kî ('because') he waits for YHWH to answer. The verb yāḥal (hophal hôḥāltî, 'I wait with hope') from ḥil ('to writhe, wait in tense expectation') indicates patient but urgent hope. YHWH himself — ʾattâ taʿăneh ('you will answer') — is the one who speaks in the courtroom where humans have heard only silence. The logic: be silent before false accusers; wait for the True Judge to answer.
16: kî-ʾāmartî pen-yiśmĕḥû-lî bĕmôṭ raglî ʿālay higdîlû (kî-ʾāmartî pen-yiśmĕḥû-lî bĕmôṭ raglî ʿālay higdîlû): 'For I said, 'Only let them not rejoice over me, who boast against me when my foot slips!'' The internal monologue kî-ʾāmartî ('for I said') reveals the psalmist's specific fear: pen-yiśmĕḥû-lî ('lest they rejoice at my expense'). The condition bĕmôṭ raglî ('when my foot slips') — the very stumbling that Psalms 37:24 promises YHWH prevents — is here the feared occasion for enemy gloating. The phrase ʿālay higdîlû, 'they magnify themselves against me,' describes arrogant self-assertion at the psalmist's expense. The prayer for non-shame (cf. Psalms 25:2; Psalms 35:19) underlies the entire penitential complex.
17: kî-ʾănî lĕṣelaʿ nākôn ûmaʿkōbî negdî tāmîd (kî-ʾănî lĕṣelaʿ nāḵôn ûmaʿkōḇî neḡḏî ṯāmîd): 'For I am ready to fall, and my pain is ever before me.' The phrase lĕṣelaʿ nākôn ('prepared for limping/stumbling') — the participial nākôn ('prepared, established') with ṣelaʿ ('limping, stumbling') — describes permanent precariousness: the psalmist is perpetually on the verge of collapse, one more failure away from falling. The phrase ûmaʿkōbî negdî tāmîd, 'and my pain is ever before me,' means the suffering is never out of sight: it is the constant foreground of experience, not the background. The word maʿkōb ('pain, anguish') appears in Isaiah 53:3 as the Servant's defining experience: 'a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.'
18: kî-ʿăwōnî ʾaggîd ʾeddĕʾag mēḥaṭṭāʾtî (kî-ʿăwōnî ʾaggîd ʾeddĕʾag mēḥaṭṭāʾtî): 'For I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin.' The decisive turn: the psalmist moves from describing suffering to explicit confession. The verb nāgad (hiphil ʾaggîd, 'I will declare, confess') marks the public acknowledgment of ʿăwōnî ('my iniquity'). The parallel ʾeddĕʾag mēḥaṭṭāʾtî, 'I am sorry/troubled because of my sin,' uses the verb dāʾag ('to be anxious, worried, sorrowful') — not mere intellectual acknowledgment but emotional distress over the sin itself. This is the movement of genuine repentance: from suffering → recognition of sin's role → active confession → sorrow. The penitential psalm reaches its theological core.
19: wĕʾōyĕbay ḥayyîm ʿāṣēmû wĕrabbû śōnĕʾay šeqer (wĕʾōyĕḇay ḥayyîm ʿāṣēmû wĕrabbû śōnĕʾay šeqer): 'But my enemies are vigorous and many, and those who hate me wrongfully have multiplied.' The contrast: the psalmist is diminished (Psalms 38:3-10), but the enemies are ḥayyîm ʿāṣēmû ('lively, vigorous and strong'). Their number has rabbû ('multiplied') — a qualitative increase in opposition. The phrase śōnĕʾay šeqer ('those who hate me falsely/without cause') returns to the Psalter's recurring motif of unprovoked enmity (cf. Psalms 35:19; Psalms 69:4 — both cited in John 15:25 for the world's hatred of Jesus).
20: wĕmĕšallĕmê rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ yiśṭĕnûnî taḥat rŏdpî-ṭôb (wĕmĕšallĕmê rāʿâ taḥaṯ ṭôḇâ yiśṭĕnûnî ṯaḥaṯ rŏḏpî-ṭôḇ): 'Those who render evil for good accuse me because I follow after good.' The specific charge against the psalmist: mĕšallĕmê rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ ('those who repay evil for good') — the same moral inversion as Psalms 35:12. The word śāṭan ('to be an adversary, to accuse') appears here in verbal form: yiśṭĕnûnî, 'they act as adversaries against me / they accuse me.' The ground of their accusation: taḥat rŏdpî-ṭôb, 'because I pursue good.' Goodness is the provocation: the wicked cannot tolerate virtue in others because it judges them by contrast.
21: ʾal-taʿazbēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî (ʾal-taʿazbēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî): 'Do not forsake me, O LORD! O my God, be not far from me!' The negative imperatives ʾal-taʿazbēnî ('do not forsake me!') and ʾal-tirḥaq ('do not be far!') are the central petition after the long confession and description of suffering. The prayer against divine abandonment (ʿāzab) picks up the physical abandonment of strength (Psalms 38:10, kōḥî ʿăzābānî) — even the psalmist's own strength has forsaken him; now he prays YHWH will not do the same. The spatial nearness ʾal-tirḥaq echoes Psalms 22:11 and Psalms 35:22 — the same prayer in the darkest moments of the Psalter.
22: ḥûšâ lĕʿezrātî ʾădōnāy tĕšûʿātî (ḥûšâ lĕʿezrātî ʾăḏōnāy tĕšûʿāṯî): 'Make haste to help me, O Lord, my salvation!' The urgent final petition ḥûšâ ('hurry!') from ḥûš ('to move quickly, hasten') adds temporal urgency: help is needed now. The address ʾădōnāy with the possessive tĕšûʿātî ('my salvation') makes YHWH personally the psalmist's yešûʿâ — salvation personified. The brief ending stands in sharp contrast to the extended suffering of Psalms 38:1-20: the long description of distress yields to a two-element prayer — come to my help quickly, you who are my salvation. The compression is itself a form of trust: the psalmist reduces everything to the one thing needed.
